title: Heart Fasting / 心齋
summary: A Zhuangzi concept of spiritual purification through emptiness of the heart-mind, taught by Confucius to Yan Hui as the path to navigate the human world.

title: 序分 / Buddhist Prologue Section
summary: The introductory section of Buddhist sutras establishing credibility and context for the main teachings.

title: Swimming Through the World with an Empty Self / 虛己以遊世
summary: A Zhuangzi parable about how one who empties oneself of ego and self-interest becomes invulnerable to external harm.

title: Xuandao (The Mysterious Way) / 玄道
summary: The supreme metaphysical principle "玄" (Xuan/Mystery) discussed in Baopuzi, described as the origin of nature and the great source of all myriad things, transcending physical existence.

title: Yan Hui / 颜回 / 顔回
summary: Confucius's most beloved disciple, renowned for his wisdom, virtue, and contentment in poverty

title: The Yan Person Returning Home / 燕人歸國
summary: A story from Liezi about a man from Yan who was raised in Chu and became confused about his identity when returning to his homeland.

title: Yang Zhu's Philosophy in Liezi / 楊朱篇的哲學思想
summary: Yang Zhu's individualist and hedonist philosophy as presented in the Liezi, emphasizing self-preservation, the brevity of life, and rejection of fame and social obligations.

title: Intention as Dust, Mind as Transitory / 意者塵往來爾
summary: The philosophical concept from Guanyinzi that intention (意) is merely the comings and goings of dust, while the mind (心) remains fundamentally unchanged.

title: From Love Arises Sorrow; From Love Arises Fear / 由愛故生憂 由愛故生怖
summary: A canonical Buddhist gatha stating that attachment is the origin of suffering and fear, and liberation comes from detachment.

title: Yu (Dwelling/Abode) in Daoist Philosophy / 寓（道家哲學中的棲居）
summary: The concept of "寓" (dwelling/abode) as the Dao's manifestation in all things, from Chapter 2 of Guǎn Yǐnzǐ.

title: Yu Gong Moves the Mountains / 愚公移山
summary: A famous Chinese fable from Liezi about an old man who decided to move two large mountains blocking his village, demonstrating the power of persistence and collective effort.

title: Yu Ye Nü Jing (Sutra of Lady Yu Ye) / 佛說玉耶女經
summary: A Buddhist sutra teaching conjugal conduct to Lady Yu Ye, featuring the famous doctrine of Seven Types of Wives.

title: Moonlight Youth (月明童子) — Protagonist of the Moonlight Bodhisattva Sutra / 月明童子——《月光菩薩經》的主人公
summary: The Moonlight Youth (月明童子) is a clean-minded young man addressed by the Buddha, symbolizing purity of mind in the sutra.

sources.title: The four books : Confucian analects, the great learning, the doctrine of the mean, and the works of Mencius (p.481-510)

title: Moon-Topped Tathāgata / 月上如來
summary: Moon-Topped Tathāgata (月上如來) is a historical Buddha described in the Mahāyāna Dhāraṇī of Compassion, predecessor of the Lotus-Up Tathāgata.

title: Cloud Ladder / 雲梯
summary: An ancient Chinese siege weapon, a heavy mobile tower used to scale city walls, against which Mozi developed sophisticated defensive strategies.

title: Zhang Liang's Daoist Cultivation and Exile / 张良修道绝谷
summary: Zhang Liang (Zhang Liang), famous strategist of the Han founding, abandoned worldly affairs to pursue Daoist cultivation and practices of fasting (绝谷), demonstrating historical evidence for immortality cultivation.

title: Will and Passion-Nature / 志與情的關係
summary: Mencius's doctrine on the hierarchical yet interactive relationship between will (zhi) and passion-nature (qing)

title: Yan Chengzi's Nine-Year Spiritual Progression / 顏成子游九年大妙
summary: The account of Yan Chengzi You's nine-year spiritual transformation from worldly refinement to ultimate enlightenment, as recorded in Zhuangzi.

title: The Deformed Ones / 支離其形
summary: Zhuangzi's celebration of physically deformed individuals who, excluded from worldly usefulness, survive and prosper—the paradox of form versus spirit in Daoist philosophy.

title: Zhongni at Home / 仲尼燕居
summary: A chapter from the Book of Rites where Confucius discusses ritual propriety (禮), the principle of moderation (中), and the complementary roles of ritual and music (禮樂) with his disciples.

title: Robber Zhi Chapter (盜跖) / 盜跖篇
summary: Zhuangzi Chapter 29 presents a fictional dialogue between Confucius and Robber Zhi, featuring radical critiques of Confucian morality, civilization, and social order.

title: Zhuangzi Chapter 27: Fables / 莊子·寓言
summary: The 27th chapter of Zhuangzi introducing three types of discourse—fables, elders' sayings, and spontaneous speech—to convey Daoist teachings through external examples and natural patterns.

title: The Fisherman Chapter (Yu Fu) / 莊子·漁父
summary: A Zhuangzi chapter featuring a Daoist fisherman who teaches Confucius about authenticity (真), criticizing artificial ritual and social conventions while advocating naturalness and genuine sincerity.

title: Gengsang Chu Chapter (庚桑楚) / 庚桑楚篇
summary: Chapter 23 of Zhuangzi featuring the disciple Gengsang Chu's philosophy of self-preservation, his dialogue with Laozi on the "hygiene of life," and profound discussions of being and non-being.

title: "Great Skill Appears Clumsy" / 大巧若拙
summary: A famous Zhuangzi paradox stating that true mastery looks awkward or clumsy, while apparent skill invites theft and competition.

title: Zhuangzi Chapter on Heaven and Earth / 莊子·天地
summary: A key chapter from Zhuangzi exploring the nature of the Way, governance through non-action, and the corruption of innate nature through artificiality.

title: Kè Yì (Cultivating Deliberate Purpose) / 刻意
summary: Zhuangzi Chapter 15 explores five types of self-cultivation and advocates for the sage's path of tranquility, simplicity, and non-deliberate action.

title: Shanxing (Rectifying Nature) Chapter in Zhuangzi / 莊子·繕性
summary: A chapter discussing the rectification of nature through stillness and naturalness, contrasting worldly learning with returning to original spontaneity, and analyzing the historical decline of the Dao.

title: Supreme Joy (至樂) in Zhuangzi / 莊子至樂篇
summary: Zhuangzi's philosophical exploration of true happiness beyond worldly pleasures, arguing that ultimate joy transcends conventional notions of joy itself.

title: Zhuangzi's "Thieving the Box" / 莊子·胠篋
summary: A Zhuangzi chapter arguing that preventive measures against petty theft enable great thieves, and that sage teachings are exploited by those who steal states.

title: The Operations of Heaven (Tian Yun) / 天運第十四
summary: A philosophical chapter from Zhuangzi exploring natural spontaneity, supreme benevolence, Daoist music, and critiques of Confucian ritual propriety.

sources.title: The four books : Confucian analects, the great learning, the doctrine of the mean, and the works of Mencius (p.481-510)

title: The True Painter / 眞正的畫者
summary: The Zhuangzi story of a painter who achieved perfect spontaneity by removing all conscious thought, embodying Daoist non-action in artistic creation.

title: Zhuangzi's Turtle Story / 莊子曳尾塗中
summary: The famous anecdote of Zhuangzi refusing official service from the King of Chu, choosing instead to drag his tail in the mud—a classic expression of Daoist values.

title: Zhuangzi Chapter 7: Responses of Kings / 莊子·應帝王
summary: Chapter 7 of Zhuangzi discusses the qualities of the ideal ruler and the principle of non-action in governance, featuring the famous allegory of Chaos (渾沌).

title: Zhi Bei You (Northern Journey of Knowledge) / 知北遊
summary: Chapter 22 of Zhuangzi exploring the nature of knowledge and the omnipresence of the Dao through dialogues between Zhi, Wu Wei Wei, Kuang Qu, and Huang Di.

title: Zigong / 子贡
summary: A prominent disciple of Confucius known for diplomatic skill and commercial success.

title: Zilu / 子路
summary: A prominent disciple of Confucius known for his boldness and loyalty.

title: Satori / 悟
summary: Satori is a Japanese Buddhist term for a sudden awakening or enlightenment experience, often associated with Zen practice.

title: Seven Factors of Enlightenment Commentary (An Shigao) / 七覺意注（安世高）
summary: An early Chinese commentary by An Shigao on the seven factors of enlightenment within the thirty‑seven bodhiparamitas.

title: Righteousness / 义
summary: A fundamental Confucian virtue representing moral righteousness, appropriate conduct, and the principle of doing what is right.

title: Five Roots and Five Powers Commentary (An Shigao) / 五根五力注 (安世高)
summary: Commentary on the Five Roots and Five Powers by the early Chinese Buddhist translator An Shigao, forming part of the systematic exposition of the thirty‑seven bodhipāramitās.

title: 仁愛 / Benevolent Love
summary: A Confucian virtue of genuine, substantive love for all people, foundational to moral cultivation and interpersonal harmony.

title: Ren'ai / 仁爱
summary: The supreme virtue of humaneness and compassion in Confucian philosophy, representing the foundation of ethical behavior and ideal human relationships.

title: 仁者 / Benevolent Person
summary: A person who has cultivated and embodies the Confucian virtue of benevolence (仁), serving as a moral exemplar.

title: 儒家 / 儒家
summary: Confucianism - a foundational Chinese philosophical tradition centered on ethics, virtue, and social harmony.

title: Confucian Self-Reflection / 儒家自省
summary: A Confucian practice of daily moral introspection for cultivating virtue and self-improvement.

title: Junzi / 君子
summary: The Confucian ideal of a morally cultivated person who embodies virtue and proper conduct.

title: Eight Tathāgata Stupas / 如來八寶塔 / 如来八宝塔
summary: The eight sacred stupas representing key events in the Buddha's life, described in the Mahāyāna Mind-Ground Contemplation Sūtra

title: Zigong / 子貢
summary: A prominent disciple of Confucius known for diplomatic skill and commercial acumen.

title: Mencius's Political Philosophy / 孟子的政治哲学
summary: ... (one line)

sources.chapter: 公孫丑上Gong Sun Chou I
sources.title: 孟子 · 公孫丑上Gong Sun Chou I

title: Mind-Ground Dharma Gate / 心地法門
summary: A central Buddhist practice of cultivating and contemplating the mind-ground as the basis of spiritual awakening.

title: The Man Who Worried About Heaven Falling (杞人憂天) / 杞人憂天
summary: The famous anecdote about unnecessary worry over things beyond one's control, illustrating the Liezi philosophical approach to existential anxiety.

title: Birth through Suffering, Death through Comfort / 生子憂患死於安樂
summary: A philosophical concept from Liezi expressing that hardship produces growth while comfort leads to decline.

title: 關尹子 / Guanyinzi
summary: A Daoist text attributed to Yin Xi, presenting teachings on sage governance and cosmic transformation
